URL: https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa1614954

SUMMARY:
The bioresorbable scaffold was associated with a higher incidence of device thrombosis than the metallic stent through 2 years of follow-up.

enriched_points.expanded_content: The patient cohort included 35 individuals with complex coronary artery disease, indicated by J-CTO scores ≥2, highlighting significant procedural challenges.
Percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) utilized bioresorbable scaffolds, guided by advanced intravascular imaging techniques such as IVUS and OCT, ensuring optimal scaffold placement.
All patients achieved procedural success with no immediate complications; however, longer-term follow-up indicated higher rates of device thrombosis compared to metallic stents.
The study underscores the need for careful patient selection and monitoring for potential complications with bioresorbable scaffolds, reinforcing the importance of ongoing research.
